planting.note: Plant cuttings 12–18 inches long in well-drained, sandy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun after last frost; red dragon fruit requires a trellis or support structure for its vining habit. In zone 9, provide frost protection below 32°F.

directions: Plant cuttings in well-drained, sandy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun in zones 10–12, with trellis support; red-fleshed dragon fruit differs from the common white-fleshed variety (S. undatus) in producing fruits with vivid deep magenta to crimson flesh — dramatically more colorful and considered by many to have superior flavor with sweeter, slightly more complex notes. The spectacular, white, night-blooming flowers up to 12 inches across are among the largest of any cactus, pollinated by moths and bats. Like all pitayas, this species vines with aerial roots along support structures and requires staking when young. The red-pigmented flesh contains high levels of betacyanins — potent antioxidants responsible for the vivid color. Multiple self-fertile clones exist; 'American Beauty' and 'Physical Graffiti' are popular red-flesh varieties. Needs at least 2 years from cuttings to first fruit; grafted plants begin producing sooner.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors in spring in very well-drained, sandy, neutral to alkaline cactus mix; transplant into the garden when several inches tall. In zone 8-9, site in the warmest, most frost-protected microclimate available. Organ pipe cactus is slowest to mature from seed; cuttings root more readily.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, sandy or gravelly, neutral to slightly alkaline, lean soil in full sun in zones 9–12; organ pipe cactus is a multi-stemmed columnar cactus native to the Sonoran Desert of Arizona and Sonora, Mexico — the signature species of Organ Pipe Cactus National Monument. The dark green, 20+ ribbed columns eventually reach 15–20 feet in height in a candelabra form. The night-blooming, pale lavender to pink-white flowers are pollinated by long-nosed bats, which migrate northward from Mexico specifically to feed on cactus blooms. The reddish-purple, spiny fruits — the pitaya dulce (sweet pitaya) — are among the most important traditional foods of the Tohono O'odham people of the Sonoran Desert, eaten fresh or dried into fruit leather. A freeze-sensitive cactus (damaged below 26°F) best suited to the warmest, driest areas of zone 9 and the true desert Southwest.

planting.note: Plant stem cuttings in very well-drained, sandy, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun after last frost; this species is faster-growing than most columnar cacti and will begin producing flowers and fruit within 3–5 years from cuttings.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, sandy to rocky, neutral to slightly alkaline, lean soil in full sun in zones 9–12; the 'Queen of the Night' name is shared with several night-blooming cacti, but this species — the monstrose cereus sold commonly in garden centers and grocery stores as 'peruvian apple cactus' (now distinguished from C. repandus) — is a large, multi-ribbed columnar cactus from South America that becomes a substantial, multi-stemmed specimen in frost-free conditions. The large, white, intensely fragrant flowers open at night for a single evening in summer — a dramatic event attracting moths and night-pollinators. The fruits that develop are round, red to yellow, with white, sweet, refreshing flesh surrounding black seeds, eaten raw in South America where they are called 'pitaya' or 'tuna.' More cold-tolerant than most tropical cacti, this species survives brief temperatures down to about 25°F in zone 9.

planting.note: Allow stem segments to dry for 2–3 weeks before planting in very well-drained, dry, alkaline to neutral, sandy or rocky soil in full sun; tree cholla is among the hardiest of the cholla cacti, surviving to zone 5 in dry, well-drained conditions.

directions: Plant in dry, well-drained, rocky or sandy, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; tree cholla is the most architecturally striking of the upright cholla cacti, producing a distinctive, branched, tree-like form with heavily ribbed, cylindrical joints covered in sheathed, retrorsely barbed spines. The magenta-pink flowers in late spring are surprisingly large and showy for a cholla, and the yellow, spine-covered, persistent fruits remain on the plant through winter — attractive and wildlife-valuable. This is among the cold-hardiest cholla cacti, native to the Great Plains and Chihuahuan Desert from Colorado and Kansas south to Mexico, and survives temperatures as low as zone 5 in dry soils. The densely spiny structure provides excellent nesting habitat for curve-billed thrashers and cactus wrens. The dried skeleton of old plants — the woody, latticed, hollow tubes — is used in crafts and decoration. An outstanding drought-tolerant specimen for dry, sunny gardens from zone 5 to 11.

planting.note: Plant stem cuttings in fertile, moist to well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; wax rose pereskia is shade-tolerant for a cactus and grows naturally in humid tropical forests. In zone 9, grow as a container plant moved indoors in winter or in frost-protected positions.

directions: Plant in fertile, moist, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun in zones 10–12; wax rose pereskia is a leaf-bearing cactus from Central America and the Caribbean — one of the few cacti that retains broad, true leaves and tolerates partial shade, making it appear almost non-cactus-like in growth form. The large, waxy, orange-pink flowers are strikingly beautiful and ornamental. Throughout Southeast Asia and tropical America, the tender young leaves are consumed as a nutritious leafy vegetable — boiled briefly and eaten as a pot herb or raw in salads. The leaves are high in antioxidants and have been studied for anti-inflammatory properties in traditional Malay medicine. Unlike most cacti, it grows in humid tropical conditions with partial shade, making it a unique edible cactus suitable for shaded, moist positions in tropical gardens. The leaves have a slightly mucilaginous texture when cooked, similar to okra.
